results.scenario_name: Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection
results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Shipment and Parcel clearly distinguished as aggregate root and child entity with separate lifecycles. (2) Shipment status explicitly called out as derived/computed from Parcel statuses in BR-3 and the ubiquitous language — the critical domain insight is front and center. (3) Eight parcel-level states defined (CREATED, PICKED_UP, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, DELIVERED, FAILED_ATTEMPT, LOST, RETURNED), exceeding the 4-state minimum. (4) Business invariants cover partial delivery detection (BR-4), lost parcel escalation (BR-7), parcel-level disputes (BR-6), and order completion gating (BR-9). (5) Eight non-trivial edge cases surfaced including lost-then-found (carrier reports delivery for LOST parcel), concurrent scan events/race conditions, split delivery addresses, and single-parcel shipment degenerate case. (6) Twelve detailed acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format.

observations.text: The derivation rule (BR-3) is stated as an invariant with explicit justification that there is 'no setter for Shipment status' — this is the critical domain insight and it is unambiguous.
BR-4 provides detailed derivation formulas for ALL_DELIVERED, PARTIALLY_DELIVERED, IN_TRANSIT, FAILED, and CREATED, with concrete examples of parcel combinations.
State machine (BR-5) covers happy path and failure paths with explicit terminal states (DELIVERED, RETURNED) and the FAILED_ATTEMPT retry loop.
Edge case for concurrent scan events correctly identifies optimistic locking and timestamp-ordered processing — addresses the race condition concern.
Dispute granularity is correctly scoped to individual Parcels (BR-6, AC-5), not Shipments.
Business impact section adds stakeholder analysis and risk assessment beyond what was required, showing deep domain understanding.
Confidence section honestly flags low-confidence areas like retry thresholds and dispute policy for LOST parcels, showing intellectual honesty rather than false certainty.

The edge case for lost-then-found rejects the transition as invalid rather than exploring a recovery workflow — while defensible, the analysis could acknowledge that a business process for parcel recovery may be needed.
No explicit mention of idempotency for duplicate carrier scan events — the concurrent events edge case addresses ordering but not exact-duplicate handling.
Time window for partial delivery escalation (how long to wait before notifying customer of partial delivery) is not addressed.

results.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring from shipment-level tracking to parcel-level tracking to support partial delivery detection. Currently, a Shipment is a single unit with one tracking number and one linear status (PENDING -> CONFIRMED -> SHIPPED -> DELIVERED). The business needs each Parcel within a Shipment to have its own independent lifecycle so that partial deliveries, individual parcel losses, and granular customer disputes can be detected and resolved.","bounded_context":

meta.notes: Preliminary scoring by Coach K. Human review pending via web app at localhost:3000.
